phrasalVerbs.definition: To stop asking for something or saying that you will do something because a lot of people oppose you.
examples: The government refused to back down on the controversial policy.
She never backs down from a challenge.
After weeks of protests, the company finally backed down.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To start dealing with someone or something much more strictly.
examples: Police are cracking down on drink-driving.
The school is cracking down on bullying.
Authorities have started to crack down on tax evasion.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To stop competing or arguing and accept that you cannot win.
examples: After hours of negotiation, they finally gave in to our demands.
Don't give in—keep fighting for what you believe in.
She refused to give in to pressure from her colleagues.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To criticise someone who has criticised you; to deliberately hurt someone who has hurt you.
examples: The politician hit back at critics during the press conference.
The company hit back with a statement defending their practices.
She hit back at the accusations in a lengthy social media post.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To speak angrily to or against someone; to try to hit or attack someone suddenly and violently.
examples: He lashed out at reporters for invading his privacy.
She tends to lash out when she feels threatened.
The cornered animal lashed out at anyone who came close.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To put someone in a prison; to confine or imprison.
examples: The criminals were locked up for twenty years.
They should lock up people who commit such serious crimes.
He spent five years locked up before being released.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To keep treating someone badly or unfairly, especially by criticising them.
examples: Why do you always pick on your younger brother?
She felt that her manager was picking on her unfairly.
Stop picking on him—he's doing his best.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To keep telling someone what to do in an unfair or unpleasant way.
examples: I won't let anyone push me around.
The older employees were pushing the new recruits around.
She's tired of being pushed around by her supervisor.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To not allow yourself to be treated badly, especially by someone in authority.
examples: She finally stood up to her bullies.
It takes courage to stand up to injustice.
He stood up to his boss about the unfair working conditions.

fillInExercises.sentence: Despite public pressure, the minister refused to ___.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Back down' means to withdraw from a position under pressure.

fillInExercises.sentence: The authorities are ___ down on illegal parking.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Crack down on' means to deal more strictly with something.

fillInExercises.sentence: After a long dispute, the union finally ___ to management's terms.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Give in' means to accept defeat or stop resisting.

fillInExercises.sentence: The actress ___ back at the tabloid's false claims.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Hit back' means to respond to criticism with counter-criticism.

fillInExercises.sentence: When confronted, he ___ at everyone in the room.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Lash out' means to react angrily or attack suddenly.

fillInExercises.sentence: She was determined not to be ___ around anymore.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Push around' means to bully or dominate someone.

fillInExercises.sentence: Why do they always ___ the quietest person in the group?
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Pick on' means to repeatedly target someone unfairly.

fillInExercises.sentence: It's important to ___ up to discrimination.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Stand up to' means to confront and resist unfair treatment.

lines.speaker: Priya
lines.text: I feel like my supervisor is constantly [pick on] me.

lines.speaker: Dan
lines.text: You shouldn't let anyone [push around] you like that.

lines.speaker: Priya
lines.text: I tried to [stand up to] her, but she just [lash out] at me.

lines.speaker: Dan
lines.text: Don't [give in]. HR should [crack down (on)] this behaviour.

lines.speaker: Priya
lines.text: I hope they don't expect me to [back down].

lines.speaker: Dan
lines.text: If she [hit back] unfairly, document everything.

lines.speaker: Miguel
lines.text: The opposition [hit back] hard during the debate.

lines.speaker: Sophie
lines.text: Yes, but the prime minister refused to [back down].

lines.speaker: Sophie
lines.text: Critics say he's just [pick on] political rivals.

lines.speaker: Miguel
lines.text: The public wants leaders who [stand up to] powerful interests.

lines.speaker: Sophie
lines.text: Let's hope no one [lash out] and escalates the tensions.
